content.assessment: This proposal most directly confronts the equity stakes of closure by explicitly naming segregation risk and targeting compensatory support to Roma and low-income families, who constitute 45% of students in segregated schools nationally per the cited education-finance evidence and who already face starkly unequal baseline outcomes (58.7% versus 9.3% early school leaving, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08). The lens evidence on commute harms (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10) directly substantiates the proposal's claim that transport provision alone is insufficient mitigation. However, defaulting to closure as the baseline response risks accelerating the territorial concentration and ghettoization dynamic already documented as worsening (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05), and mitigation measures do not address the more fundamental sorting that occurs when public schools consolidate while church-run alternatives, serving more advantaged intakes, remain unaffected (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04).
opportunities: Statutory transport and cost-transparency reforms could be paired with explicit segregation-monitoring requirements tied to the ghetto-school concentration pattern (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)

Targeted equity support could be calibrated using existing disparity data (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09) to prioritize the most disadvantaged displaced populations
strengths: Explicitly targets anti-segregation safeguards and transport mitigation to Roma and low-income populations facing the most unequal existing outcomes (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)

Recognizes, consistent with the lens evidence on commute harms, that transport provision alone is an insufficient equity remedy and requires further downstream mitigation (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)
threats: Rural teacher shortages and quality gaps in disadvantaged schools (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09) mean that even the consolidated destination schools may not offer meaningfully better quality, undermining the proposal's implicit assumption of net gain from consolidation

Even with mitigation, closures could still contribute to demographic decline in already-declining rural areas (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01), compounding future access loss beyond what transport and funding measures address
weaknesses: Default-to-closure framing accepts continuation of the territorial concentration dynamic that is already increasing ghetto schools primarily in villages, especially North-East Hungary (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)

Does not address the differential intake advantage of church schools relative to public schools (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04), meaning consolidated public options may still lag behind alternative pathways available to more advantaged families
